methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in AI & Software (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 38 same-sector peers exist in AI & Software, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

data.narrative:
Preferred Networks is a Series D+ stage AI & Software company that has raised ¥16.88B to date. The valuation universe for this stage company spans a fragmented competitive set of 1 public comparable (Palantir, ticker PLTR) and 8 private peers including OpenAI, xAI, Anthropic, and Databricks. The broader AI & Software sector contains 38 public and private companies, but meaningful peer multiples are constrained by the scarcity of companies at identical stages with comparable capital deployment and business maturity.

The peer-median method is not applicable to this valuation due to insufficient narrow peer cohort definition. After filtering the 38-company AI & Software universe for same-stage, adjacent-stage, and similar-valuation-tier criteria, fewer than three datapoints emerged in any single filter bucket. Applying all-sector medians would introduce unacceptable noise given the heterogeneity of AI business models, margins, and revenue bases across the broader software landscape. This constraint reflects a structural challenge in frontier-tech valuation: companies at Preferred Networks' scale and funding stage operate in a sparse comparable universe where traditional triangulation methods often fail.

Without sufficient quantitative anchoring from peer multiples, valuation synthesis depends heavily on fundamental build-out metrics—capital efficiency, revenue trajectory, and path to sustainable unit economics—rather than market multiples. Key uncertainties include the company's actual revenue run-rate and margin profile relative to the top-tier private peer set, both of which are required to support any defensible valuation range.
